Real Testing Notes: Best for: Beginners wanting quick automations without technical skills. Most app marketplace.
column14: Limited to 15 steps per zap (free), gets expensive at scale ($0.004 per task overage), brittle (breaks when apps update), no complex data transformation.
column15: TESTED: Set up WordPress → Salesforce → Gmail automation in 20 minutes. Worked perfectly for 3 months, then broke silently when Salesforce API updated. Error notifications were slow (2-3 hours). Customer support was helpful but response time was 24+ hours. Great for simple automations, not for mission-critical workflows.

Real Testing Notes: Best for: Power users needing complex workflows and data transformation. Lower cost at scale.
column14: Steeper learning curve (scenarios can get complex), fewer integrations than Zapier, documentation can be confusing for beginners, error messages are technical.
column15: TESTED: Built complex lead scoring workflow with 8 apps and conditional logic. Initial setup took 4 hours (vs 2 hours in Zapier), but could do things Zapier couldn't (data transformation, error handling). Ran flawlessly for 6 months. Once understood, more powerful than Zapier. Better for complex workflows, Zapier better for quick simple ones.

Limitations: Best for: Technical users wanting full control and self-hosting. Most powerful but highest learning curve.
Real Testing Notes: Requires technical knowledge (JSON, APIs), self-hosted version requires maintenance, cloud version is newer (less mature), steepest learning curve of all tools.
column14: TESTED: Installed self-hosted version on own server (took 2 hours to set up). Built workflow that would have been impossible in Zapier (custom API calls, complex data manipulation). Performance was excellent. However, when I got stuck, community support was my only option (no official support for self-hosted). Cloud version is easier but less customizable. Amazing tool if you're technical, avoid if you're a beginner.

Real Testing Notes: Best for: Enterprises needing advanced integrations and custom connectors. Powerful but expensive.
column14: Very expensive (not for small businesses), steep learning curve, overkill for simple automations, sales process is required (no self-service pricing), focused on enterprise.
column15: TESTED: Used during enterprise pilot. Built complex integration between Salesforce, SAP, and custom internal tools. Tray.io handled scenarios that made Zapier crash. However, the $299/month starting price is hard to justify for small teams. Sales team was helpful but pushy on annual contracts. Incredible power if you have the budget, but Make/n8n offer 80% of features for 20% of price.
